did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning models (VGG16, InceptionV3, and ResUNet) and an ensemble method to classify skin cancer images from the ISIC dataset. Transfer learning was applied to VGG16 and InceptionV3 for feature extraction and classification of seven skin cancer categories.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the training and evaluation of deep learning models (VGG16, InceptionV3, and ResUNet) on the ISIC dataset to classify skin cancer. It includes specific methodology (preprocessing, feature extraction, ensemble approach), results (accuracy, sensitivity, etc.), and a conclusion based on new findings, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Skin Cancer Detection using VGG16, InceptionV3 and ResUNet
journal: Proceedings of the Fourth International Conference on Electronics and Sustainable Communication Systems (ICESC-2023)
brief_summary: This study addresses skin cancer classification by utilizing an ensemble of three deep learning models: VGG16, InceptionV3, and ResUNet. The researchers trained these architectures on a dataset of 10,500 dermoscopic images from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC), covering seven distinct skin cancer categories. Preprocessing steps included color normalization, resizing to 224x224 pixels, and data augmentation techniques such as rotation and flipping to prevent overfitting. Transfer learning was applied to VGG16 and InceptionV3 to enhance feature extraction and classification performance. The models were evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, F-score, and precision, with individual accuracies of 82.5%, 85.8%, and 81.9% respectively. The ensemble method aggregated the results of the three models, achieving superior performance compared to any individual model and demonstrating commendable efficacy in precisely categorizing skin cancer.

all_task_types.target: Seven distinct skin cancer categories (basal cell carcinoma, seborrheic keratosis, melanoma, benign keratosis, actinic keratosis, vascular lesions, and dermatofibroma)
all_task_types.details: The paper uses VGG16, InceptionV3, and ResUNet models to classify dermoscopic images into discrete diagnostic categories. The study employs an ensemble method to aggregate classification results, achieving an accuracy of 93.7% on the ISIC dataset. Although ResUNet is typically a segmentation architecture, in this context, it is utilized within the ensemble for classification purposes alongside VGG16 and InceptionV3.

datasets_used.name: International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC)
datasets_used.description: A public dataset of dermoscopic images of skin lesions gathered by the ISIC, accessed via Kaggle. The paper notes it contains about 10,000 to 10,500 images with comments for associated diagnosis.

use.training: Used to train the VGG16, InceptionV3, and ResUNet models. The dataset was divided into 'train' and 'valid' directories.
use.validation: Used for validation during model training (referenced as 'valid' directory and validation loss/accuracy graphs).
use.testing: Not explicitly reported as a separate test set; the 'valid' directory was used for validation. The paper mentions the dataset is divided into 'train' and 'valid' directories used to train and test models, but specific test counts are not provided.
use.other: The dataset was preprocessed (color normalization, resizing to 224x224) and augmented (rotation, flipping, magnification) to prevent overfitting and balance classes.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, F-score, precision) and compared qualitatively to other models.

ISIC (International Skin Imaging Collabo.dataset_description: Dataset of 10,500 dermoscopic images covering 7 skin cancer categories (basal cell carcinoma, seborrheic keratosis, melanoma, benign keratosis, actinic keratosis, vascular lesions, dermatofibroma). Divided into 'train' and 'valid' directories.
ISIC (International Skin Imaging Collabo.split: Training and Validation (used for testing individual models and ensemble)
ISIC (International Skin Imaging Collabo.notes: The ensemble model outperformed all individual models across all metrics. The paper notes that individual models achieved high accuracy and low loss on training and validation datasets, indicating no overfitting. The ensemble approach used weights calculated based on each model's performance on the validation dataset.

architecture_used.main_model: Ensemble of VGG16, InceptionV3, and ResUNet
architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning applied to VGG16 and InceptionV3 using pre-trained weights (weights frozen during initial building); ResUNet described as a hybrid architecture combining residual connections and U-Net, originally designed for remote sensing but used here for classification.

VGG16.layers: 16 layers total (13 convolutional layers, 3 fully connected layers)
VGG16.components: Input layer, convolutional layers, ReLU activation layer, pooling layers, hidden layers, fully connected layers

InceptionV3.components: Mix of symmetric and asymmetrical features (average bundling, turrets, max bundling, concates, abandonment pads), fully connected layers, Batchnorm, Softmax

ResUNet.type: Encoder-decoder Deep Residual UNET (Hybrid of U-Net and Deep Residual Learning)

training_details.loss_function: Softmax (for InceptionV3); Accuracy and Loss curves monitored for all models
training_details.hyperparameters: Batch size, learning rate, and number of epochs were varied through multiple experiments to enhance performance
training_details.training_schedule: Transfer learning used; weights frozen for pre-trained models initially; new dense layer appended for classification; performance examined on validation set; weights calculated for ensemble based on validation performance

classification_or_output_head.type: New dense layer appended for classification (for VGG16/InceptionV3); Weighted Average Ensemble of decision maps (probability distributions) from all three models
classification_or_output_head.classes: 7 skin cancer categories (basal cell carcinoma, seborrheic keratosis, melanoma, benign keratosis, actinic keratosis, vascular lesions, dermatofibroma)

preprocessing_techniques: Color normalization (normalizing images to have unit variance and zero mean)
Image resizing (scaling to a constant dimension of 224x224 pixels)
Data augmentation (rotation, randomised flipping, and magnification to avoid overfitting and enlarge the dataset)
Skin segmentation (separating the relevant region in the photos to avoid class imbalance and ensure equal amounts of photographs per group)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to the dataset to avoid overfitting and enlarge the dataset. Specific angles or probabilities were not reported.

Skin segmentation was used to separate the relevant region in photos. To avoid class imbalance, the dataset was balanced to ensure every group had an equal amount of photographs.

conclusions: The proposed ensemble method combining VGG16, InceptionV3, and ResUNet demonstrates commendable performance and is a promising approach for skin cancer diagnosis.
The ensemble model achieved superior performance compared to individual models, with an accuracy of 93.7%, sensitivity of 0.97, specificity of 0.96, F-score of 0.91, and precision of 0.97.
Individual models achieved accuracies of 82.5% (VGG16), 85.8% (InceptionV3), and 81.9% (ResUNet), but the ensemble approach significantly boosted performance.
The weighted average ensemble technique ensures robust and accurate predictions by weighting model contributions proportional to their validation performance.
Transfer learning reduced training time and improved the performance of the VGG16 and InceptionV3 models.
The models did not overfit the training data, as indicated by high accuracy and low loss on both training and validation datasets.
The achieved results are satisfactory in comparison to other contemporary techniques.
Incorporating decisions from multiple individual learners is essential for improving performance and accuracy in delicate matters like skin cancer diagnosis.
important_remarks: The paper claims the ISIC dataset contains 'nine classes' in the Conclusion section, but the Abstract, Introduction, and Methodology sections consistently state it contains seven classes; this is a significant internal inconsistency regarding the dataset structure.

The study relies entirely on the ISIC dataset accessed via Kaggle, which may introduce population bias (e.g., overrepresentation of certain demographics or imaging devices common in the ISIC archive) and lacks external validation on independent clinical datasets.

ResUNet, originally designed for image segmentation in remote sensing, is repurposed here for classification without detailed justification for its architectural suitability compared to standard classification CNNs.
The paper does not report specific hyperparameters (learning rate, batch size, number of epochs) or the specific optimizer used, limiting reproducibility.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) is provided to validate the superiority of the ensemble model over individual models.

The dataset was artificially balanced by ensuring 'every group had an equal amount of photographs' via skin segmentation and sampling, which does not reflect the natural prevalence distribution of skin lesions in clinical practice and may lead to overoptimistic performance estimates.
The 'valid' directory was used for both validation during training and final testing, raising concerns about data leakage or lack of a truly held-out test set for final evaluation.

The authors acknowledge the need for future work on interpretability (explaining what features the models learn), indicating the current model acts as a 'black box' with limited clinical trustworthiness.
